revision.text:
[[arz:ضريح اساكوسا]][[de:Asakusa-Schrein]][[en:Asakusa Shrine]][[es:Santuario de Asakusa]][[fa:معبد آساکوسا]][[fr:Asakusa-jinja]][[id:Kuil Asakusa]][[ja:浅草神社]][[ko:아사쿠사 신사]][[pl:Asakusa-jinja]][[ru:Храм Асакуса]][[th:ศาลเจ้าอาซากูซะ]][[uk:Святилище Асакуса]][[zh:淺草神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Asakusa district, Tokyo, Japan|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Asakusa Shrine
浅草神社 | image = File:Asakusa shrine 2012.JPG | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.715138888889|N|139.79743611111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767|1=Shinto}} | type = | deity = {{ill|Haji no Nakatomo|qid=Q123219617|1=Haji no Nakatomo}}; {{ill|Hinokuma no Hamanari|qid=Q123219585|1=Hinokuma no Hamanari}}; {{ill|Hinokuma no Takenari|qid=Q123219599|1=Hinokuma no Takenari}} | founded_by = {{ill|Haji clan|qid=Q10927758|1=Haji clan}} | established = 1649 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-26-1, [[Asakusa]], [[:en:Taitō, Tokyo|Taitō-ku]]
{{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} 111-0032 | website = {{URL|http://www.asakusajinja.jp/english/|qq=}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|qid=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Asakusa|qid=Q129328405|1=Asakusa}} | part_of = {{ill|Q11557223|qid=Q11557223|1=Q11557223}} }} {{nihongo|'''Asakusa Shrine'''|浅草神社|Asakusa-jinja|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the [[Asakusa]] district of {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Also known as {{nihongo|''Sanja-sama''||Shrine of the Three gods|qq=}}, it is one of the most famous Shinto shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=http://www.mytravelguide.com/attractions/profile-79065205-Japan_Tokyo_Asakusa_Shrine.html |title=Asakusa Shrine |accessdate=2008-03-07 |work=MyTravelGuide| archiveurl= https://web.archive.org/web/20080306000841/http://www.mytravelguide.com/attractions/profile-79065205-Japan_Tokyo_Asakusa_Shrine.html| archivedate= 6 March 2008 | url-status= live|qq=}} The shrine honors the three men who founded the neighboring [[Sensō-ji]]. Part of a larger grouping of sacred buildings in the area, Asakusa Shrine is on the east side of the Sensō-ji, down a street marked by a large stone ''[[torii]]''. One of the only two buildings in the area to survive {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, it is designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} due to its long history. ==History== [[File:AsakusaShrine1439.jpg|left|thumb|A ''[[torii]]'' marking the entrance to Asakusa Shrine]] An example of the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|gongen-zukuri]]'' style of architecture, Asakusa Shrine was commissioned by {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and built in 1649 during Japan's {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3001.html |title=Sensoji |accessdate=2008-03-06| work=japan-guide.com| archiveurl= https://web.archive.org/web/20080306034657/http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3001.html| archivedate= 6 March 2008 | url-status= live|qq=}} It was constructed to honor the three men who established and built the Sensō-ji. Legend states that two brothers, fishermen named Hinokuma Hamanari and Hinokuma Takenari, found a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=bosatsu|1=Bodhisattva}} [[Kannon]] statuette caught in a fishing-net in the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|qid=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}} on May 17, 628.{{cite web |url= http://www.worldgreatestsites.com/sensoji.htm |title=Asakusa Kannon (Sensoji Temple) |accessdate=2008-03-06 |work=World Greatest Sites |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20080318103100/http://www.worldgreatestsites.com/sensoji.htm |archivedate=18 March 2008 |url-status=dead |qq=}} The third man, a wealthy landlord named Haji no Matsuchi (Haji no Nakatomo), heard about the discovery and approached the brothers to whom he delivered an impassioned sermon about the Buddha. The brothers were very impressed and subsequently converted to the Buddhist religion. The Kannon statue was consecrated in a small temple by the landlord and the brothers who thereafter devoted their lives to preaching the way of Buddhism. This temple is now known as the Sensō-ji. Asakusa Shrine was built in order to worship these men as deities. The shrine and its surrounding area and buildings have also been the site of many Shinto and Buddhist festivals for centuries. The most important and famous of these festivals is {{ill|Sanja Matsuri|en|Sanja Matsuri|qid=Q219122|lt=Sanja Matsuri|1=Sanja Matsuri}}, held in late May. Unlike many other structures in the area, including the Sensō-ji, the shrine (along with the nearby Nitenmon gate to the Sensō-ji) survived the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|Tokyo air raids of 1945]]. Because of this rich history, it was designated an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the [[Government of Japan|Japanese Government]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=http://www.asakusajinja.jp/asakusajinja/syaden.html |script-title=ja:社殿について |accessdate=2008-03-21 |language=Japanese|qq=}} Buildings and structures in addition to the main shrine include a ''[[kagura-den]]'' (''[[kagura]]'' dance hall) and the Hikan Inari Shrine. A Nishinomiya Inari shrine was once located near the {{ill|Hōzōmon|en|Hōzōmon|qid=Q2378896|lt=Hōzōmon|1=Hōzōmon}} gate to Sensō-ji.{{cite web |title=Event: Praying for Success in Business at Asakusa Hatsuka-Ebisu 2023 |first= |last= |date=23 January 2023 |url= https://everywhere.tokyo/en/event-asakusa-hatsuka-ebisu-2023/ |work=Tokyo Shitamachi Guide |publisher= |access-date=13 April 2024|qq=}}McClain, James L., John M. Merriman, Kaoru Ugawa and Ugawa Kaoru. (1997). [https://books.google.com/books?id=-qOuykzxhKUC ''Edo and Paris: Urban Life and the State in the Early Modern Era.''] Ithaca: {{ill|Cornell University Press|en|Cornell University Press|qid=Q2997531|lt=Cornell University Press|1=Cornell University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-8014-8183-3|qq=}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/39088759 OCLC 39088759] [https://books.google.com/books?id=-qOuykzxhKUC&dq=Sensoji&pg=PA403 p. 403.] After the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Meiji government|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} ordered the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in 1868, the Nishinomiya shrine became part of the Asakusa Shrine and was located near the ''kagura-den'', where it was destroyed in the 10 March 1945 firebombing. ==References== {{Commons Category|Asakusa shrine|Asakusa Shrine|qq=}} {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Shinto shrine|qq=}} {{Authority control|qq=}} [[en:Asakusa Shrine]] [[arz:ضريح اساكوسا]] {{Commons category|Asakusa shrine}} [[de:Asakusa-Schrein]] [[es:Santuario de Asakusa]] [[fa:معبد آساکوسا]] [[fr:Asakusa-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Asakusa]] [[ja:浅草神社]] [[ko:아사쿠사 신사]] [[pl:Asakusa-jinja]] [[ru:Храм Асакуса]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าอาซากูซะ]] [[uk:Святилище Асакуса]] [[zh:淺草神社]] {{wikidata link|Q670049}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taito ward]] [[Category:明神]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:東京都区部の重要文化財]] [[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:江戸時代の建築]] [[Category:浅草の歴史]] [[Category:浅草寺]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]]
